speaker: interviewer
text: --is 72, which it isn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Susan, can you say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to be starting shortly. We are in Skokie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And do you mind to say something so I can check the mic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm Judy Lachman. I was born in Poland, in Tomaszow Mazowiecki, in 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Awesome.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: June 29, 1995; Judy Lachman; interviewer, Susan London; region, Chicago, Illinois, United States of America; in English. My name this is Susan London, L-O-N-D-O-N. It's June 29, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Judy Lachman, in Skokie, Illinois, United States of America. Would you tell us your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Judy Lachman, L-A-C-H-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judyta Strykowska, J-U-D-Y-T-A, S-T-R-Y-K-O-W-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 1, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Tomaszow Mazowiecki-- T-O-M-A-S-Z-O-W dash, capital T-- excuse me, capital M-A-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Poland-- in central Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what your life was like before the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was part of a family of four-- my father, Joseph Strykowski, my mother, Gitel Strykowska, and I had a brother, three years older than I. And we lived in a middle town-- Polish middle town in Poland. Our-- we were better than average situated, because my father was a wholesaler of glass, china, earthenware, and window glass. And in Poland, there was-- there were cartels. So he was the sole distributor of those factories for 10 towns. And even though there was a lot of anti-Semitism in Poland, and some people boycotted the Jewish business, nolens volens, whether they wanted or not, they had to come and buy by us. And our business was, naturally, closed on Saturday. And if somebody came after sundown, they had to wait til after Shabbat to come and take care of their business. We are-- we were a modern orthodox family with a great appreciation of everything that was Jewish and cultural. My parents were very much pro-Israel, but they did not believe in going to Israel. They were hoping, when-- when Moshiach will come, we will all go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is Moshiach?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to Palestine, at that time. Life was good-- carefree. I was fortunate to attend a private school-- had my friends. And when I was 12 years old I realized that Poland-- even though it was a country of my forefathers for generation-- did not quite accept us as equal citizens. As a very strong impression on me made a incident-- this was-- I was at that time not quite 12 years old, and we had a parade in our school. And my brother was carrying the Polish flag on the streets of our town. And a hoodlum threw-- a Polish hoodlum-- threw a knife right through his earlobe. He could have killed him-- that a Jew should not carry a Polish flag. This kind of changed my outlook, and I became very strongly attached to a Zionist organization. The Zionist organization's name was Akiba. And a lot of my friends belonged there too. And it took up-- took up a lot of my social life and energy. And being that my mother was busy in the business, and my father was travelling a whole week, that filled in quite a lot of my time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When-- in 1939, during the summer vacation, there was already starting a-- a talk-- a talk about a possibility of war was starting. Of course, before that, there were news in the papers about the prosecution of the Jews in Germany. But, of course, we were people of hope, and we always thought that this is over there. And nothing is going to come to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe what you were hearing about Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did believe, because it was in the [NON-ENGLISH] that was such a-- weekly that showed it. We-- it was also the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] was writing. And some people got the news-- the German newspaper, and so on. Of course, we believed. But it was not something that we thought it's the end of the world. It was very disturbing and very bad. But deep inside, we kind of trusted that the German people are cultural people, and they are not going to be led so much astray.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and when there was talk about war-- I remember it was a summer evening. We were sitting in a park with a group of young people. I said, how can there be a war? We still have invalids from the first world war, and people still remember those terrible hardship. And why would the world get involved in another war?

speaker: interviewee
text: And pretty soon, we got up in the morning, and I thought it was a thunderstorm. And little did I realize that this was already bombing of my city before the war was officially declared. And there were also, at that point, victims a block away where the bomb hit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go any further into the war itself, could we back up a little and talk a little bit more about your prewar life? What was your daily life like in your town-- for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in my daily life, I attended school. We-- I was a very sheltered child. I was very close to my family and my father. And we were involved with holidays. And Shabbat was a big thing. Because being as my father was away during the week, he came home, and we did a lot of family-- good time together. We-- we-- we sang together zemirot. We had visitors. And my mother and my father believed that the good Lord gave them to distribute in a good way. And my father was very charitable. In fact, he belonged to a shtiebel, which was a little house of worship, and-- where he supported-- practically-- the rabbi. And people, wanderers, came there from other town. He never came home from a prayer to leave there somebody that didn't have where to go for the next meal, or where to spend the next night. He felt very compassionate. He was very sensitive to people's need-- if they needed a loan-- of course, without any interest-- just to help out. And he was very, very fond of children, and so was my mother. But evidently, they had a few miscarriages. And beside my brother and me, there were no other children. The family used to get together for simchat, which means for joyous occasions-- for weddings, for birthdays. And the majority of my father's family lived in Piotrkow, which was a town about 30 kilometers from my hometown. And my mother's family lived in Radomsko, and other nearby towns. Both my mother and my father come from a family of seven brothers and sisters. And in my mother's family, they had two children each. In my father's family, they had eight, six, five, seven. Life was just, in general, wonderful. I used to love reading. This was one of my favorite pastimes. I belonged to the school choir. I was active in-- beside the Zionist organization, I was active in the Red Cross. And life was carefree and good. I will say it again. I was 14 years old when the war broke out and changed my whole life. Right away, there was no school for the Jewish children. I was, again, fortunate to attend private classes-- small groups. In fact, in one year, I made two school years. I just was going to start the third [NON-ENGLISH]. And beside working on my own school work, the organization that I belonged to had organized schools for children. And each of us had a group. And I had a group of 10 children. And I was teaching them, like, first grade-- to read and write. And it was very satisfying to see those children starting to read and write. The heartache was that many of them came very hungry to the lessons. We met at each other's homes-- of the children. And that I couldn't help so much-- just a little bit. Because once the Germans closed the ghetto in 19-- the end of 1940, '41, food was a very scarce thing. People that still had some belongings sold the-- some of them for exchange of food. We were in a ghetto where they didn't give us any rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the year before the ghetto was closed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was that when you were teaching also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We just started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that legal?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't legal. And the point I wanted to make-- before the war, my parents weren't really very excited about me belonging to a Zionist organization. They were afraid that I'm going to get so dedicated, and I might leave home. So they tried very much to discourage it. But during the war, my parents allowed to me to have meetings in our house, even though a meeting caught would have resulted in destroying the whole family. But they still believed that this was important. And especially, there were-- the-- in the evening, we couldn't walk. There was-- I forgot how it was called.

speaker: interviewer
text: A curfew?

speaker: interviewee
text: A curfew, thank you. It was a curfew. So sometimes, we even had a [NON-ENGLISH], which means we changed the night to day, and we spend a whole night together reading, and singing, and reading poetry, and dreaming about a better future. This was the spiritual resistance. And if we wouldn't have that spiritual resistance, you couldn't exist. This really brought you down very quickly if there was nothing to look forward to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that before September of 1939 there was bombing in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the morning of September 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the war wasn't yet officially declared at that time. So it was a cloudy morning, and we thought that this was a thunderstorm. But later on, in the afternoon, we realized that this was already war starting. And we all were very kind of frightened. So we decided to pack our belongings and go towards Warsaw-- that maybe there is going to be a front that the Germans are not going to be able to come through so easily. And so being as we were in business, and we had those big wagons where the glass was transported from town to town, and we had four horses-- so a friend of my father took one wagon. And we, with some friends, took another wagon with-- that was like on rubber wheels where the riding was more comfortable. And we put a lot of our mobile belongings on that wagon. And we went about 35 kilometers from home. And there was a lot of traffic. And there was a fork in the road. And one wagon went one direction. And one wading-- wagon went the other direction. In the same day we were minus all our belongings. And we were going towards Warsaw with thousands and thousands of other people. And my father, being as he dealt with a lot of people in surrounding town, had friends in each town. Until we came to a town close by Warsaw-- and my father said, we are not going any farther, because it's going to be very, very bad with everybody going in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was called [NON-ENGLISH], that town. This is a small town before Warsaw. Because my father's [NON-ENGLISH] was between Warsaw, Piotrkow, and Tomaszow Mazowiecki-- all those towns in between. And there we stood a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: In one week, Poland was conquered till Warsaw. And later, a front was by Warsaw for weeks. And everything was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go instead of Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, we went back home. When we came home, we practically don't had-- didn't have a change of clothes. Because we took everything that was good with us. But we were very fortunate that we had a big magazine of window glass. And being as the majority of windows were knocked out by the bombing, we kind of came a little back financially, and we bought all kind of necessary clothes and so on. In fact, I kind of felt bad to have new clothes with all my friends not having a means to live. Especially I had children of the professors that were my friends, and other professional people, and so on. And they lost their job. And there was no way to buy bread. And there I was in-- in new clothes. That was kind of embarrassing. But I needed clothes. And as I said, we-- were kind of in a shock. But we still thought that it's going to be a blitzkrieg. A blitzkrieg is called that it's going to just pass through very fast. And as the war was going on, different decrees came out against the Jews. First, they wanted us all to congregate in one part of town. Later on, they surrounded-- they closed it by buildings-- by-- by-- let's say, this and this building was the end where Jews couldn't go any farther. And then they had different gates and walls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was there for a long time-- until 1941-- the beginning. I was still in our house from before the war-- in our apartment. But in 1941, the-- there was junk collectors that the Germans needed the junk. So they encouraged that the people should go out and collect all the iron and other things. And this was-- was giving the people a means so to go out, not only to collect the junk, but maybe bring home a little food or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- it was a very-- very valued thing to be in-- involved in it. And the people that, even before the war, lived in the building where I lived, which was a very big building, like a town. Everything was in that building-- a shoemaker, and a tailor, and a milkman with cows, and a-- I don't know-- a guy that makes horseshoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just everything. And there was also those junk collectors had a magazine there. So they later took over the whole building with those people that they choose to go out and collect the-- the junk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later on, we moved, like across the street in a old house with a garden. And we had a-- a very small room that was partitioned. And we lived there with my brother, and father, and mother. And my old grandmother lived with us, too. And we were still fortunate that we had that little-- those little quarters all to ourself, to our family, because other families had to double up and live with two, three families. Was it your choice to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't our choice, because we needed to go out of the apartment to vacate it for those junk collectors. And this was a project that the Germans supported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to take whatever you wanted of your belongings with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- oh, at that time, we could take a little bit. But these were such tiny quarters, that all there was place was for beds and for couches. And there was a little partition like, where there was a tiny little kitchen. There was really no place for many furniture to-- to-- to take. And as time goes-- went on, anything that was valuable, we used to sell for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you moved, were you compensated for the move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We this-- nobody was compensated for anything during-- you were lucky that you were let go. Many nights, the Germans made auctions. And they gathered intelligentsia, or they gathered certain professional people. And in the morning, they were all found shot. The ghetto was run by a Jewish Judenrat, which is like, I would say, like a federation. And the Germans were giving orders. Like, this day, they need so and so many people to come to work. They wanted to deliver all the jewelry, or deliver all the fur coats-- all those things. Or if they needed to send out some people for work-- you know, like slave labor-- they gathered the young people. Or-- and they sent them out of town. And some of them never, never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life wasn't in our hands. Life was in the Germans' hands. We did our best to preserve our humanity the best way we could. But-- What did you do for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: For that, as I mentioned, we educated ourself as young people. We helped to educate the younger than us. We had those meetings that we gave each other spiritual support. The organization that I belonged to had the headquarters in Krakow. And now and then, we got a representative that came with news. And then we realized what was happening outside. And as time was going on, the situation was getting worse and worse. Jewish communities in small towns were forced to join in the larger-- larger towns so that everybody will-- would be grouped together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we stood in my town. And my town was gathering the people from the surrounding little towns. Before the war, there were 15,000-- 45,000 people in general. It was an industrial town. And there are 15,000 Jews. We lived pretty much close to each other before the war, with the exception some people lived a little farther. But the majority of the Jewish population lived in one section. There were special schools, public schools, supported by the government, that were strictly Jewish schools. And there were-- supported by the government-- Catholic schools. We did not go to school on Saturday, but we went Sunday to school. And the Catholic people did not go Sunday, so it worked out good for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So what-- what did you spend your time talking about during 1940 and '41, with your family and your friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were hoping that the war is going to end. We-- we were still hoping for a-- for the best future. We didn't give it up till-- till 1941, '40. We thought that we are going to stick it out, so to say. And we were giving each other courage in the-- in the youth group. We also organized a very original thing. We-- and this was kind of suggested by the movement I belonged to. We had like a square block. It was like a garden, but it was fenced around. And there, we put-- and it was-- we-- it was under the assumption that this is a farm re-education, that the people were teaching us farming. And-- and this was just an undercover for our activities. And in that garden, way by the fence-- down there-- we dug out like a 15 meter hole in the ground. And you went down there. This was like in a circle-- 15 meters. And you went down there. And you could sit around. There could be 25 people, 30 people sitting down there in the hole. And when you passed by the street, you thought that the garden was empty. And we had vegetables growing there, and--
